segments.text: [MUSIC PLAYING]

NOAM GEFEN: Hello, and
thanks for joining us today.

I'm Noam, a product manager
for Android for car developer
platforms.
And I'm joined by Sole and Ben.

We're here to share
some exciting updates

and opportunities
across Android for cars.

Google has been investing
in automotive experiences
since 2010.

And the industry has not
only evolved considerably

during that time, but
the pace of innovation
continues to accelerate.

We've evolved our
automotive offerings

to adapt to the demands of
car owners, car manufacturers,

and developers like you
and continue to double down
on the in-vehicle experience.

While our products
and cars have evolved,

our mission remains to
build a safer, more seamless

and helpful connected
driving experience,

whether you access
that experience

through your phone via
Android Auto projected

or directly from the car
display through Google built in.

This mission guides the
work we do every day,

and we're excited to
show you how we can help.

Modern car cabins are
changing for the better.

New screens and more
capable computing power

affords more opportunities
for car developers

to create experiences for
both drivers and passengers.

Today, we're going to share more
about the ways we've been trying

to help you bring your ideas
and innovations to cars,

and the timing
couldn't be better.

Virtually all new cars sold
today support Android Auto,

with almost 250 million
compatible cars on the road
today.

And the number of cars
with Google built in
is growing rapidly too.

A great lineup of more
than 50 car models

are already on the road, and
more are coming this year.

A thriving developer
ecosystem is

paramount to
delivering capabilities

that the car drivers and
passengers are looking for.

Users and automotive
partners have told us

that they want more
app experiences.

The ecosystem of available
apps has grown significantly.

And with your help, we
are excited to continue
this momentum.

While apps are
helpful while driving,

they can keep you entertained
and productive while parked.

With many more streaming and
gaming options available,

you can enjoy the time
you spend charging your EV

or waiting for
curbside delivery.

We'll share more about
this in a moment,

but the bottom line is that it's
an exciting time for the app
ecosystem in cars.

So now I'll hand it over to
Sole to talk about major updates
coming soon to cars.

SOLE ALBORNO: Hey,
everyone, I'm Sole.

I'm a product
manager for Gemini.

I'm excited to talk
to you about how

we are bringing this
revolutionary new technology
to cars.

Gemini is your AI assistant from
Google, and it's coming to cars.

And with it, we are introducing
a whole new set of experiences.

Gemini has the power to help
you safely multitask and make

your drive more productive
and even more enjoyable,

while keeping your eyes
on the road and your hands
on the wheel.

Talking to Gemini is really easy
because you can speak naturally

to help without having to
remember specific phrases
or commands.

And while you are
on the road, Gemini

allows you to take action
and handle more things
from your favorite apps.

It can help you brainstorm,
take notes, and find inspiration

on the go to make every journey
more productive and enjoyable.

To give you an idea
of what this means,

imagine you are driving
through a new town,

and both you and your
electric car need to recharge.

Gemini can find
charging stations

where you can get
a cup of coffee

too and help you
navigate there with ease.

Have you ever been in
a rush to get somewhere

and realize you forgot
to check the location?

Well, instead of stopping
and grabbing your phone,

you can ask Gemini to get
the location from your email
and then start navigating there.

There is another technical
session on the subject

that you can check out called
finding the perfect Gemini

fit on Android, and it's
available to stream now.

Our goal is to make it easier
to integrate with Gemini.

So if you are building
a navigation app,

we have introduced three
core intent formats

to trigger navigation,
display search results,
and execute custom actions.

An example of this
would be, users

can ask Gemini to start
navigating to a place

or use their voice
to report incidents

like heavy traffic,
road closures, and more.

The navigation intents are
now available to integrate.

And we are working with
several app developers

to unlock Gemini power
app interactions.

We are also bringing
Gemini Live to the car,

so you can have a
conversation about things
that are on your mind.

For example, you can ask Gemini
for a list of parts and tools

you might need for a DIY
project you are doing while you
are on the way to the store.

Or you can brainstorm some ideas
for your next family vacation.

There is so much you
can do with Gemini,

and it will be rolling out to
cars over the coming months.

Now I'm going to
hand it back to Noam

to share the latest on some
of our developer programs
and opportunities.
NOAM GEFEN: Thank you, Sole.

The Gemini era in cars
sounds very exciting.

Let's switch gears
and talk about how

you can make sure to bring
the best version of your app
experiences to cars.

Last year, we introduced
car app quality

tiers to inspire
developers to create
high-quality in-car experiences.

We have the Car Ready tier
that relies on the Android app

compatibility across all form
factors that allows you to bring

your mobile apps on
both Android Auto

and cars with Google built in
for a tablet-like experience
while parked.

If your mobile app is a
video, gaming, or browser app

and is already adaptive
for different screen sizes

and orientations, it is likely
to meet the Car Ready quality

tier with almost no
additional effort.

The Car Optimized
quality tier guides

apps that feature customizations
specifically for in-vehicle use.

Depending on the app's category,
developers using the car app

library can build
tailored app experiences
suitable for driving.

Developers can also build custom
parked experiences as well.

And finally, we have the Car
Differentiated quality tier.

Apps following the
guidelines in this tier

can unlock experiences utilizing
the uniqueness of the car

hardware and screens, as
well as build experiences

that transition
between both parked
and driving modes in cars.

One example would be showing
turn-by-turn navigation
in the instrument cluster.

If you want to learn more
about which tiers might be best

for your app, the car app
quality guidelines have been

updated across all
categories and tiers

to align with the
latest infotainment

hardware and software
advancements,

empowering you to
build more innovative,
high-quality app experiences.

Ben will fill you in on all
of the details in just a bit.

Now that we've
covered quality tiers,

it's time to talk
about the various app
categories and their status.

With strong user and developer
momentum for Android Auto

and cars with Google
built in, we're

working to support the
same type of experiences
across both platforms.

The weather category
is now open,

allowing any developer to deploy
their weather app to production,

both on Android Auto and
cars with Google built in,

following the car app
quality guidelines.

Next, we're really
excited to announce

that we now support the game
category on Android Auto.

For Android phones running
Android 15 and above,

users can now find some
of their favorite titles,

like Angry Birds 2, Farms Hero
Saga, Candy Crush Soda Saga,

and Beach Buggy
Racing 2 to pass time

or clear some challenging levels
on the car screen while parked.

This category is now in
beta, and app developers

can submit their games for close
testing to both Android Auto
and cars with Google built in.

To publish your app to
the production track

while the category
is in beta, you

can sign up to express
interest by using this QR

code on the screen or the link
provided in the description.

We anticipate opening the
category for any developer

to distribute their adaptive
games to production, adhering

to the car app quality
guidelines to both Android Auto

and cars with Google built
in the coming months.

Lastly, we've been
seeing huge demand

from users for enabling
video apps on Android Auto.

We plan to enable this
starting with Android 16
on select compatible cars.

If your app is already adaptive,
distributing the same apps

to cars for both Android Auto
and cars with Google built in

will involve almost
no incremental cost.

To learn more about
adaptive apps,

you can watch the Android
keynote where this is discussed.

We are pleased to
expand the app ecosystem

by bringing more
categories of apps to cars

while ensuring a
high-quality bar

and will continue to open
up more throughout the year.

So stay tuned to
for updates from us.

If you would like to explore
distributing your app that

does not belong to our
supported categories,

please share your interest
by scanning this QR code

and completing the form to
let us know your use case.

We'd be happy to work
together to evolve the app
ecosystem for cars.

We introduced the car-ready
mobile apps program last year

to automatically opt
in adaptive mobile apps

from the video, browser,
and games category

to the automotive form
factor via Play Console.

This easily brings
apps to the cars

by letting them run in a
compatibility mode while parked.

We partnered closely with teams
to implement compatibility mode

to bring in more than 100
car-ready mobile apps to over

a million cars and continue
to scale with even more OEM
partners.

Through this program, we've been
able to bring some amazing app
experiences like these.

Throughout 2025,
we expect more cars

to offer car-ready
mobile app for users

to stay entertained
while parked.

Starting on cars with Google
built in running Android 14,

we are working with OEMs to
add additional mobile app

compatibility for
large screens and grow

the car-ready mobile
app catalog to thousands
of large-screen-optimized apps.

Volvo EX90 will be the
first to offer this expanded
catalog later this year.

And we are working
closely with more car OEMs

to bring the best of Google's
Play Store to more cars.

We hope the opportunity to
distribute your adaptive Android

apps to cars has
piqued your interest.

To help you build and distribute
magical and optimized car

experiences for
drivers and passengers,

Ben is going to walk you
through some of the latest tools
and updates.
BEN SAGMOE: Thank you, Noam.

It's exciting to see the growth
in every part of the Android
for cars ecosystem.
Hello.

My name's Ben, and I'm a
developer relations engineer
working on Android for cars.

Today, I'll be walking
through the many ways

we're improving the experience
of designing, building, testing,

and distributing
your app for cars.

Let's start with designing
your app for cars.

We know cars can be a unique
and challenging form factor
to design and develop for.

That's why we maintain the
car app quality guidelines

and tiers, to help you
understand not just

the minimum
requirements, but to also

inspire you to make your app's
in-car experience the best it
can be.

We've updated our car
app quality guidelines

to simplify existing
requirements

and introduce new ones that
will enable more premium
and useful in-car experiences.

As an example, we have
relaxed the requirement

to support both ARM
and x86 APIs when

distributing your app to
cars with Google built in.

Check out the latest car
app quality guidelines here
by scanning this QR code.

With the challenges
faced with designing apps

for a variety of screen sizes
and configurations in cars,

we want to make this
even easier for you.

So stay tuned for
updates to our Design

for Driving website, which can
be found using this QR code.

To give you a little taste
of what we're working on,

we published a Figma
kit to help you
prototype your apps in Car UI.

To begin with, this kit
includes a variety of components

for the templates
provided by the Android

for Cars app library, but
that's just the start.

We'll continue to
update it in the future

to make it easier to design all
types of in-car experiences.

Next up, let's discuss
all the new ways

you can bring your
designs to life.

To get started, let's look
at some platform updates
for cars with Google built in.

First off, we're expanding
Google Play services

for in-vehicle systems to
bring them on par with mobile.

14 new services have already
been launched in the last eight

months, with an additional 18
planned in the coming months.

This includes Google Play Games
Services, which is available now

and makes it easier
for your users

to sign in and continue playing
from where they left off

on another device with
Google Play Game Services.

Later this year,
we'll be bringing

in passkeys and credential
manager APIs for you

to support an even easier
sign-in flow for your users.

Lastly, Quick Share
will start rolling out

later this year to enable smooth
cross-device sharing of app

content from mobile
devices to cars.

With Quick Share,
drivers and passengers

can easily share navigation
routes, including adding stops,

or share their favorite
songs, playlists, and videos

from their preferred apps
from other Android devices
to the app running in the car.

If your car app already supports
handling intents for web links,

including Android app
links, it will automatically

work with content sent
using Quick Share.

For media apps and apps built
using the car app library that

don't already support
web links, we've

published guidance
to help you build

seamless, cross-device
sharing experiences.

We're also enabling
audio-while-driving experiences

for video apps on cars
with Google built in.

You can now implement
seamless transitions

from park video playback
to audio-only listening
during driving.

Suppose you're streaming
a live sports event
while getting curbside pickup.

Now you can continue
listening to the audio
when you start to drive again.

This is coming to cars with
Google built in running Android
14 and above.

To get started, we have
released emulator images

with this feature enabled
running Android 14 and above.

For implementation
details, refer

to the updated documentation
on developer.android.com,

including the build
and test a parked app

for Android
Automotive OS Codelab.

This feature will launch
as an early access program.

Interested developers should
apply via the provided form.

If you're a media
app developer, you

know how apps have
been evolving,

from simple music playback to
offering a more diverse range

of content, like
podcasts and audiobooks,
with more interactive features.

Recognizing this
evolution, we remain

committed to empowering
you with the capabilities

to bring these advanced,
engaging experiences
to the in-car environment.

We're doing that by prioritizing
both safety and the ability

for your app to do
what makes it unique.

Today, we are excited to
unveil the next generation
of in-car media experiences.

Collaborating with
developers, we've

recognized the need for enhanced
flexibility and customization

beyond the current
media app framework.

To address this, we're
introducing support

for building media apps using
the Android for Cars App

Library, similar
to how we support

navigation and other
car-optimized experiences that
minimize driver distractions.

This provides a new alternative
to the existing media

browser service approach,
which will continue

to remain fully supported on
both Android Auto and cars
with Google built in.

Spotify will be building their
next-gen experience, powered

by the latest APIs and templates
in the car app library.

This includes experiences
like Spotify Jam that

will be available on Android
Auto in the coming months

and on cars with Google
built in at a later date.

The new car app library changes
includes two new templates.

The media playback
template allows

you to build your Now
Playing view with the ability

to add custom interactions,
while the sectioned item

template allows you to build
discovery and catalog browsing

experiences that
minimize distractions.

Together with other templates
available like the tab template

and sign in template,
media app developers

can now build both
richer and more complete

experiences that users are
used to on their phone.

These new templates
are available today

in the 1.8 alpha
version of the library

and are currently
supported on Android Auto.

Support for them on cars
with Google built in
will follow later this year.

We expect even more top apps
to bring these new capabilities

to their media
experiences in cars.

We have also made
improvements on the APIs

needed to build better and safer
communication app experiences.

The car app library lets
you provide features

like full message history,
upcoming meetings lists,
rich in-call views, and more.

And last year, we worked with
several communications apps

to launch car app library
powered experiences

to enable both calling and
messaging experiences on the go.

Based on their feedback, we
have simplified our calling

integrations for developers
by utilizing the new calls
manager Jetpack API.

This new library will improve
your app's calling experience

across Android devices,
including allowing calls

to be answered in cars and
easier audio routing controls.

Both of these car
app library powered

experiences for media
and communications

apps are supported on
Android Auto today.

The new APIs and features
for communication apps

are available for
developers in beta,

and we encourage you
to express interest

in being an early adopter
using this QR code.

Comprehensive development
guides are now

available on
developer.android.com.

When you are getting ready
to test these features,

we've got a few major
updates to announce
that will help with this.

We know that having physical
test devices is important,

and we're happy to announce
that the Android Automotive

OS for Pixel Tablet
system image is now

generally available for
all developers to use.

Going a step further,
you can really

streamline your
in-car app testing

with the latest system image
that now supports Android Auto,

enabling you to test both
your Android Automotive OS

and Android Auto experiences
on the same device.

To make this resource
as helpful as possible,

we're making sure that the
latest updates and newest

features for cars are
released at the same time

to our emulators
on Android Studio,

as well as Android Automotive
OS images for Pixel tablets.

To sign up for access,
check out the documentation
at developer.android.com.

We're also working to help
you test your apps on real car
hardware.

To enable that, we've been
working with the Firebase team

to add Android Automotive OS
devices to the Firebase Test Lab
catalog.

For the first time,
you'll be able to test

on real car hardware without
having to own or rent a vehicle.

Access will first be available
through an early access program,

so please express your
interest by filling out

the form at this QR code or from
the link in the description.

Last but not least, once you're
done building and testing

your app's in-car experience,
we've made it easier than ever

to distribute it
via Google Play.

For apps in the park
categories, it's

now possible to distribute
the same APK or app

bundle to both phones and
cars with Google built in.

You can do this by setting
the required attribute,

as seen on the screen, to
false within your app manifest.

Building off of
that, we've also made

it possible to use the mobile
release track to distribute

these apps to cars
with Google built in,

making it easy to keep
your app updates in sync
across form factors.

This added flexibility
gives you more options

to customize, build,
and release workflows

to suit your
organization's process.

To catch issues before your
app gets distributed widely,

we've been working with the
Google Play Console team

to add pre-launch report support
for Android Automotive OS.

Expect to see these
reports later this year.

We covered a lot here, but you
can always go check out the

What's New page in the Cars
section of developer.android.com
to learn more.

Today we shared our vision
for seamless and intuitive car

experiences, previewed
future Gemini innovations,

and presented
immediate opportunities

to take advantage of
more refined quality

tiers, enhanced developer
tools, expanded app categories,
and new capabilities.

Please use the link provided
in the description below
to begin your journey.

On behalf of our presenters
and the Android for Cars team,

we thank you for your
commitment to improving
the in-car experience.

We're eager to see the
delightful and transformative

app experiences you'll create
as we drive the car app
ecosystem forward together.

Until next time, we'll see
you out there on the road.
